critic_raw: Third pass. The design uses macro-F1 as primary metric "because stakeholders require that rare, clinically important codes receive equal weight." But in a multi-label setting with 200+ codes, macro-F1 is sensitive to the number of labels - adding more codes (especially rare ones that score near zero) will depress macro-F1 for both models. Is the macro-F1 computation stable across training and test sets given that the label set itself is defined by training frequency thresholds?
defender_raw: The label space is fixed after training-set frequency filtering (codes with >=10 training occurrences). Both training and test macro-F1 are computed over the same fixed label space. A code that scores near zero in the test set (due to rarity) does contribute to macro-F1, but this is appropriate - the hypothesis specifically targets improvement on rare codes, and near-zero performance on codes the model has seen few examples of is informative. The rare-code stratum macro-F1 is reported separately (10-50 occurrence codes), which provides a focused view of the hypothesis-relevant subset. The overall macro-F1 across all codes provides the global picture.
adjudication_raw: Run 3: No unresolved flaw. Verdict: defense_wins.
